critique.summary: I conducted a comprehensive review of the Python package, inspecting all its source files. I found multiple, significant correctness bugs across most of the modules. The issues range from incorrect initial values and inverted logic in aggregations, to wrong conversion factors and mathematical errors in the core conversion logic, to contract violations regarding precision and output formatting. The test suite is a smoke test and does not exercise the code paths where these bugs are located.

findings.description: In `total_report`, the call to `format_total` is `format_total(metres, label)`, but the function signature in `report.py` is `format_total(label, metres)`. The arguments are swapped.
The `total` accumulator in `sum_metres` is initialized to `1.0` instead of `0.0`, as stated in the docstring. This will lead to incorrect sums. For an empty list, it will incorrectly return 1.0.
The function `max_metres` returns the minimum value, not the maximum, because it calls `min()` instead of `max()`.
The conversion logic is inverted. The docstring says to multiply by the `from_unit` factor and divide by the `to_unit` factor, but the code divides then multiplies.
The function rounds the result (`round(result)`), but the docstring explicitly says "MUST preserve full precision (it never rounds)".
The `UNITS` dictionary has an incorrect conversion factor for millimetres (`mm`). It is set to `0.01` (the value for cm) instead of the correct `0.001`.
The `UNITS` dictionary has an incorrect conversion factor for kilometres (`km`). It is set to `100.0` instead of the correct `1000.0`.
The `describe` method formats the string as `"<unit> <value>"` but the docstring promises `"<value> <unit>"`. The arguments to the format string are swapped.
The alias-building loop assigns `index[alias] = alias` instead of `index[alias] = canonical`, so aliases are not mapped to their canonical unit.
The value is formatted to 1 decimal place (`"%.1f"`) but the docstring specifies 3 decimal places.
